1.label: Question 1
1.scenario: Company A is a health and care insurance company that is developing a new individual Critical Illness (CI) product with guaranteed premiums throughout the policy term. Company A will carry out full medical underwriting for the new CI product.

i.question: Describe the full medical underwriting process that Company A will carry out for its new CI product.

Indicative Solution: Stage 1 - Collection of Evidence

Company A will initially obtain evidence about the state of health of the applicant. This will allow Company A to determine if the applicant is in sufficiently good health to be accepted on standard terms, or if the applicant is not in good health the terms on which the applicant can be accepted. Company A can obtain medical evidence from a range of different sources. This will also allow Company A to identify whether the applicant has any pre existing conditions.

All applicants will fill in an initial proposal form. Which may be in paper form or it may be possible for Company A to access health records electronically, (which will speed up the application process significantly). The proposal form will have fields that request the following information: Age, Gender, Occupation, Region of residence, Drinking habits, Smoking habits, Pre-existing conditions under treatment, Other medical history.

Company A's underwriters will review the applicant's initial proposal form and consider a range of factors when assessing the applicant's initial proposal form. These can include: the state of health of the applicant; the applicant's occupation; the leisure pursuits of the applicant (e.g. dangerous sports); the applicant's normal country of residence; whether the applicant travels overseas.

Depending on the response to the applicants questions the underwriters may require the applicant to get further reports from medical doctors that the applicant has consulted. The insurer may ask that the applicant to have further medical examination carried out. It is possible that further specialist tests may be required depending on the results of the doctor's report and medical examinations.

The extent to which a company uses further medical tests, or specialist tests is likely to depend on the extent of the loss that Company A would have made as a result of mis-estimating the state of health of the applicant. Higher sum insureds may automatically require specialist medical tests or medical examination report to manage anti-selection. Company A's underwriters may also have guidance about limits in terms of case size about when particular tests or further medical examinations are required.

To counter the risk of over insurance, Company A may decide to obtain financial details about the applicant. Again, Company A's underwriters will have guidance about when financial underwriting is required.
Stage 2 - Interpretation of Evidence

Once the underwriters have sufficient information available to assess a case, the underwriters will assess the available evidence. As part of this assessment Company A's underwriters may consult: doctors specifically employed by Company A about particular medical issues the applicant has; and/or any underwriting manuals prepared internally by Company A, or by Company A's reinsurer.
Stage 3 - Underwriting Decision

Once the underwriter has reviewed all relevant information the underwriters will come up with an underwriting decision. Whether the applicant is in sufficiently good health to be accepted on standard terms. Cases that are considered to bring higher risks than acceptable at standard rates can be offered higher (loaded) premiums, where the underwriting process will determine what additional risk premium loading is necessary. If the additional level of risk is too high for an applicant, Company A could choose to not offer cover to the applicant.

For non-standard risks it is possible Company A could offer the policyholder cover but could make other changes to cover. Such as: Offer the applicant a lower benefit; Postpone cover if the period of higher risk is deemed temporary; Offer a different less risk intensive policy to the applicant; Offer the applicant a CI policy but have certain specific causes and/or conditions excluded.

ii.question:
Company A is considering adding Guaranteed Insurability Options (GIOs) to its new CI product. One GIO under consideration is an option that allows the policyholder to extend the policy term on the original expiry date at Company A's standard premium rates without supplying further medical evidence.

Set out the approach that Company A could take to price the GIO to extend the policy term.

Indicative Solution: Overall: The cost of an option is the value of the excess of the premium that should, in the light of full underwriting information, have been charged for the additional assurance over the normal premium rate that is charged. The exercise of the option by lives in poor health will generate considerable additional costs. The total expected additional costs of an option depend on the health status of those who choose to exercise the option, and the proportion of lives who choose to exercise the option. Hence the overall cost could be summarised as proportion of lives exercising option multiplied by the average morbidity cost of lives exercising option. Therefore, the approach would need development of a decrement table for lives who have not yet exercised the option, with decrements of being diagnosed with the disease or death and exercising the option (other forms of withdrawal may be included). In addition, a morbidity table for those who have already exercised the option would be built.

Probability of exercising the option: The company needs to estimate the proportion of lives exercising the guaranteed insurability option. It is unlikely that the company will have the data on guaranteed insurability options given this may be a new product feature. Therefore, they may need to get the data from external sources. These may include: From reinsurers who would have reinsured such product features before; Carrying out a survey with a pool of customers on how many would exercise such an option if they already had the option; From external consulting actuaries who might have experience on such product variants; Overall industry data if the regulator or industry associations aggregate such data. External data sources may need to be adjusted. e.g. if reinsurer data includes allowance for reinsurer's own profit/prudence/expense margins. The company would need to granulate the data into key variables especially to what extent does the probability of exercising the option vary with the age at which the first policy term is reached. The data will be adjusted to reflect the circumstances of the product or the market including the marketability of the product, underwriting terms etc.

Morbidity for those that have exercised the option: To determine the morbidity rates for lives that would have exercised the option, the company can consider the morbidity rates for the same age for those who would have entered without underwriting. Appropriate loading to the non-underwritten lives will be done to account for the fact that many lives who are in sub-standard health will choose to exercise the rates. The appropriate loadings can be provided by external reinsurers, consultants and other sources. Alternatively, the same external sources can provide morbidity rates for the lives that exercise the option.

Calculation Considerations: After building the assumptions, the next stage is calculating the expected present value of benefits payable for both the first policy and the second policy after exercising of the option. The second policy would incorporate probability of exercising the option and higher morbidity rates for those that exercise the option. The expected present value of premiums for both the first policy and the second policy under standard terms would be also calculated. The difference between the expected present value of benefits and expected present value of premiums is the cost of the option. Normally, payment of the extra premium for the option ends at the end of the first policy. Hence the cost of the option would be distributed across the first policy period as an additional premium per month or the relevant premium payment frequency.

Alternative conventional approach: Alternatively, the approach can assume that all eligible lives will exercise the option. Therefore, the morbidity experience of those who take up the option will be the ultimate experience which corresponds to the select experience that would have been used as a basis if underwriting had been completed as normal when the option was exercised. The approximate approach taken here is usually to assume that the worst option from the financial point of view of the company is chosen with probability of one.

Other Considerations: Like any other pricing, there is need to consider marketability of the option and benchmark against competition to ensure the premiums aren't too expensive or too low. Consider incorporating additional margins for expenses/commission/profit in pricing of the option. However, initial commissions/expenses will not be incurred again, making the extension more cost efficient. In practice, a simplified charging structure (e.g. flat premium loading) may be applied rather than detailed option premium rates.

iii.question:
A second GIO under consideration is an option that allows the policyholder to increase the sum assured when a particular life event (e.g. on marriage or buying a new house) occurs, which is priced using Company A's standard premium rates without supplying further medical evidence.

Explain how the pricing considerations for the option to increase the sum assured may differ from the option to extend the policy term.

Indicative Solution: Among other drivers, the drivers that affect the rate at which lives can exercise the option include the below: The number of times the policyholder gets the chance to exercise the option. Conditions attaching to exercising the option such as limiting the size of the option or restricting the choice of contracts available under the option. The term of the policy with the option. The extra cost to the policyholder who exercises the option.

The mid-term option is defined based on a particular event hence can generally limit the level of anti-selection compared to the end-term option. This is because the life events including marriage or purchase of a new house would not generally be influenced by health status of the life. In contrast, the end term option would be influenced by whether, at policy maturity, the life is in sub-standard health status hence sees value in paying standard rates.

The date of exercising the option is not fixed hence the insurer has to build probabilities of when the life event may occur. This may be available at population level e.g. using government statistical office (or equivalent) on key demographic milestones like marriages and house purchase/mortgage.

The sum assured increase also may be variable hence would also add complexity in building assumptions on the level of increase that the life may opt for. Higher levels of increase may indicate anti-selection hence may require sub-standard morbidity rates to be applied accordingly.

The number of times the policyholder can exercise the option may also be multiple given that more than two life events may occur during the same policy term. Similarly, the end term option is only exercised at a known point in time whereas the particular life event can occur at any time during the duration of the policy. Furthermore, the life events do indicate a need for additional insurance cover, hence are less likely to cause over insurance or windfall payments.

2.label: Question 2
2.scenario:
Company B is a small health and care insurance company that sells a full range of health and care insurance products. Company B's main distribution channels are:

• tied-agents
• insurance intermediaries.

Company B wishes to monitor the performance of these distribution channels in terms of:

• new business sales
• business persistency
• policyholder satisfaction.

The company has recruited a Distribution Channel Manager whose first tasks will be to investigate the performance of the distribution channels via a policyholder satisfaction survey and also assessing appropriate performance metrics.

i.question: Suggest possible performance metrics that could be used.

Indicative Solution: New Business Sales Metrics: Number of new polices sold per period (monthly, weekly, quarterly, year to date, annual), or Year on year growth of new business, or Premium value / Sum Assured of new policies sold per period (monthly, weekly, quarterly, year to date, annual). Percentage achievement of target sales by count, premium value and sum assured, or Conversion ratio – e.g. Number of policies sold as a percentage of the total number of pre-sales quotations. Average policy size (by premium or sum assured) for new business sold in a period. Distribution of new business by geographical region.
Business Persistency: Lapse / renewal rates by channel. Lapse rates by duration in force (or equivalent points. e.g. commission clawback experience).

Policyholder Satisfaction: Percentage of customers who would recommend Company B to their peers. Number of successful claims as a percentage of total claims. Duration in days between claim being received by the company and the policyholder receiving their claim payment (if the distributor is involved in the claim payment process in the territory in question). Customers Review Score or Average customer rating (e.g., 5-star, 4-star etc).

ii.question: Discuss the factors the Distribution Channel Manager should consider in designing and implementing the policyholder satisfaction survey.

Indicative Solution: Overall: The design should take into consideration the aim/objective that the company intends to achieve by conducting the survey. The questions asked should be sufficiently detailed to allow the company to get insight into the customers perceptions, preferences, and behaviours.

The company will want to track the entire customer journey when assessing the customers satisfaction. This can start with the customer's experience and satisfaction during the policy application process. Then the company can seek review of the sales process from the customer's view. The customer's experience during underwriting can also provide other areas of improvement or high customer satisfaction. Including satisfactory rating questions such as "Would you recommend the company to a peer?", or "Overall, how would you rate your experience?"

The questions asked in the policyholder satisfaction survey should enable the company to objectively calculate the policyholder satisfaction metrics it wishes to track. The questions asked should help the company clearly identify the parts of the process that are working and particular areas that are not working. By tracking customer responses over time the company can monitor whether customers responses are improving or worsening. Therefore, the platform used for the survey should promote ongoing and real-time policy satisfaction feedback instead of being once off. This should be particularly useful to track consumer responses to any service improvements or changes in approach by the company. Identify any areas company is concerned about and wants policyholder feedback on, so particular questions can be included on these areas in the survey.

Operational: The company should consider how the policyholder satisfaction survey should be asked. For example, should it be paper based, or via an electronic survey. Consideration should be given to the platform that would be most accessible to the customers. If customers generally have access to digital tools including mobile phones, electronic survey would be most appropriate. However, for developing countries where access to mobile phones and laptops is limited, a paper-based survey may be more appropriate. The company would also need to consider the expenses of each option and balance them against accessibility of the platform accordingly. In addition, the platform chosen should be in line with the culture and strategic objectives of the company. If the company is carrying out digital transformation, electronic surveys would enable the company to continue delivering digital communication platforms for customers. Some customers may find it dated and not climate sensitive if the company chooses a paper-based survey. The company may therefore be exposed to reputational risk if it proceeds with a paper-based survey. The company will want to make the survey is as simple to complete to encourage as many customers as possible to carry out the survey.

Accuracy/Clarity: The overall survey should not be excessively long or complex to increase the likelihood of customers completing the survey. The questions should be clear and as unambiguous as possible to ensure the answers are as accurate as possible. The questions asked need to be relevant and sufficiently accurate to allow it to calculate metrics it wants to measure. If the questions are not sufficiently accurate or clear, the responses from the policyholders may not show the company sufficient data to identify and improve the areas where there are issues. The company would need to make sure that the profile of the respondents is indicative of its business profile, which may mean the survey approach needs to be adapted to achieve these response rates. By including an open-end question to capture any additional information that may not have been covered by the existing questions in the survey. Request respondents' permission to follow up with further investigation to allow for more qualitative information gathering.
Complexity: The questions asked should not be overly complex or require specialist medical knowledge to be able to answer.
Legislation: Ensure that all questions in the survey are consistent with local legislation. e.g. general data protection regulation.
Industry Body: Company may decide to use policyholder satisfaction questions drafted by Industry Body to be consistent with other insurance companies in the market.

Other Considerations: The company will need to also consider the required marketing budget to ensure customers are aware of the survey and how they can be able to complete the survey. Small rewards can be considered to incentivize the customers to participate in the survey. Low participation would reduce the credibility of the results hence the company needs to ensure the response rate reaches a minimal credible volume. It is also prudent for the company to consider carrying out a pilot phase where a sample of customers test the survey and give feedback to the company. However, this needs to be managed to ensure that it doesn't result in material delays in launching the survey. Automates reminders to increase survey response/uptake rates.

iii.question:
The results of the investigation indicate that the performance of the main distribution channels is not meeting the targets set by Company B.

Describe the actions Company B could take to improve the performance of its main distribution channels.

Indicative Solution: Communications with distributions channels: Actions that the company will take will be informed by the performance gaps that were noted for the main distribution channels. The immediate action will be to open channels of communication with the distribution channels – e.g. speak to them about their performance regularly and agree mutual actions to improve performance. Feedback from the main distribution channels will enable the company to determine the best actions which can deliver the expected results. Follow up actions may be taken such as sharing the experience with the distributors and discussing any potential change required, or warning them that they need to take action or there will be consequences if the trend continues, or publishing the outcome from the investigation.

Business Mix: Company could look to discourage business from a particular distribution channel which was loss making (for example it sells business to higher risk lives) or high risk (poor persistency). This could be done by increasing premium rates for business sold by that distribution channel to discourage future business, or at least ensure that any business written by that distribution channel is not loss making. Or could encourage the channel/distributor to guide its sales towards lower risk lives. This could be done by changes in channel/distribution remuneration.
Distribution: Within a particular distribution channel only deal with a distributor that is profitable. Stop selling through unprofitable distribution channels or distributors.

Pricing and product design: Ensure the pricing assumptions for a particular distribution channel are up to date and reflect the experience for that distribution channel. To ensure that the business written is as profitable as possible. Or at least ensure that the profitability for that distribution channel was understood. Company could look to address cross subsidies in pricing. Product design could be amended if necessary to suit the differing needs of a variety of distribution channels – e.g. a more simple product may be suitable for some distribution channels.
Marketing: Ensure sufficient and appropriate marketing carried out for each distribution channel.

Competition: Ensure the product terms offered and pricing are competitive for each distribution channel that the business is sold through. If the performance is aligned to the competition, then it may be that the targets have been set at too challenging a level.

Training: The insurer could provide more and better training to a distribution channels salesforce to help them develop skills on how to provide best customer service for customers. The distribution channels can also be trained on how to profile customers and ensure they sell to lives which more closely match the risk profile of the pricing basis.

Distribution channel remuneration: It could change the commission terms for a particular distribution channel to incentivise profitable sales, and dis-incentivise unprofitable sales. It could change the nature of commission terms to encourage longer term business – e.g. less high upfront commission more trail commission. It could increase incentives for good business, such as providing better training and sales support. It could introduce (or increase) commission clawback for business from a distribution channel with particularly poor short-term persistency.
Underwriting: The insurer could strengthen its underwriting procedures for business from particular distribution channels.

3.label: Question 3
3.scenario: Company C is a large health and care insurance company specialising in Private Medical Insurance (PMI). The Marketing Director of Company C has commented that the company is behind its competitors in its use of data analytics and predictive modelling.

i.question: Suggest possible reasons why Company C may be behind its competitors in its use of data analytics and predictive modelling.

Indicative Solution: The company's data may not be easily applicable to data analytics and predictive modelling, for example: The data may not be in digital format hence is not usable for the purposes; The data may be of poor quality and hence may not be good enough to provide insight; They may have limitations regarding storage capacity.

The company's data may not be in digital format hence is not usable for the purposes of data analytics and predictive modelling. The company's data, though in digital format, may be of poor quality hence not suitable to analyse and make decisions from the resultant insights.

The company may have lagged competition in making technology investments required to support data analytics and predictive modelling capabilities. This could be due to affordability of the systems or the company's budget is allocated to other departments/ functions.
The company does not have the required skilled personnel to deliver the data analytics and predictive modelling solutions within the company.

The company lacks a deliberate strategy at Board and Management level to invest into data analytics and predictive modelling, for example: Not early adopters; Not likely to invest in technology; They may be risk averse e.g. with regard to data breaches etc.

The company may have underestimated the rate at which data analytics and predictive modelling would be adopted by competition and how the capabilities would bring competitive advantage to competitors. The company has been having other strategic priorities at the expense of data analytics and predictive modelling.

ii.question: Describe the potential benefits for Company C of improving its data analytics and predictive modelling capabilities.

Indicative Solution: Product Design: The company can use data analytics to understand the demographic profiles of customers and determine customer needs. Data analytics and predictive modelling can be used to gather market intelligence and understand customer behaviours and spending habits. Allow the company to better understand customer needs and enable the company to design relevant products that customers will like and buy. The company will also be able to personalize products to each customer's needs thus meeting customer needs better and satisfying the customer.

Product Pricing: Data analytics and predictive modelling can be used to analyse underlying risks, and determine the relevant pricing commensurate with the risks to price more accurately. The company will also be able to classify risks accordingly and achieve more accurate rating for each individual customer. This will discourage anti-selection as low-risk lives don't find the average premiums to be expensive and high-risk don't find the average premiums to be very cheap. Therefore, the company will be able to build its preferred risk profile within its risk appetite. Through detailed analysis of claims and underwriting data, the company will be able to identify emerging morbidity risk factors and update its pricing models accordingly. Through predictive modelling, the company is able to accurately predict key assumptions into the future including expenses and expense inflation, lapses and investment returns. This will improve its pricing and optimize profits for the company.

Underwriting: Analysis of underwriting data will enable the company to get insights on lead causes of morbidity and how they are distributed at population level. The company will be able to revise its underwriting procedures including what levels of cover can be onboarded without underwriting and what level of underwriting is required for each band of cover. This can enable the company to balance the underwriting expenses against the benefit of reduction in morbidity. In the same vein, the company will be able to set underwriting levels at levels that don't discourage potential customers from applying for policies thus increasing business levels.

Claims and Service Providers Management: The company will be able to identify any weaknesses in the design or operating effectiveness of the claims management processes and improve the processes to be able to pay claims faster and improve customer experience. Potential areas improvement can include: The level of claim sizes that are paid without claims assessment; Level of medical procedures that can be carried out without pre-authorization; Approval limits for payment of claims which enable as many claims to be paid on time without creating weaknesses for internal or external fraud; Departments or teams that need additional recruitment to be able to process claims on time; Level of additional training to enhance skills for the claims management team.

The company can be able to understand sources and types of claims fraud that the company is exposed to and implement corrective measures to reduce the risk. The company will also be able to then review corrective measures and determine level of effectiveness of the corrective measures. Through data analytics and predictive modelling, the company is able to determine service providers that are used by their customers and consider them for addition to the preferred provider list. The company is also able to predict growth in utilization in particular service providers and medical procedures that are becoming more prevalent. The company will therefore be able to plan and ensure the service provider network is big enough to meet customer needs and key procedures are covered under key products. The company is able to also understand claim patterns for all customers and be able to identify customers that need to be placed on managed care, require preventative treatment or need lifestyle changes. Such actions will reduce claims in the long term hence more profitability for the company. These potential data-informed adjustments will also improve customer satisfaction.

Renewals: Data analytics and predictive modelling will help the company to model and predict renewal rates for its customers. The company will be able to understand factors and drivers that influence the renewal probability in customers and identify customers who are at higher risk of not renewing. The company will therefore send renewal reminders and increase engagement with such customers at higher risk of non-renewal. Analysis of spending habits and behaviours can help the company to identify promotional items that will appeal to customers and increase their propensity to renew. The company will also be able to reward customers who renew their products through loyalty rewards. Predictive modelling may help to assess its sustainability more accurately in the long term.

Marketing: Data analytics and predictive modelling will enable the company to understand media channels that their customers access and prefer. This will enable the company to personalise advertising and marketing initiatives to customers and ensure the adverts appeal to the customers. This will increase demand for products and conversion rates from not-taken-up policies to active policies. In the same vein, the company will be able to determine marketing platforms which have the best marketing spend to customer revenue potential hence make their marketing initiatives more effective.

Assurance Functions (Internal Audit, Risk, Compliance): The company will be able to apply analytics to internal audit procedures and identify weaknesses that lead to revenue leakage, fraud, and internal control gaps. This can be done automatically and reflect recent data hence internal control will be able to improve in real-time. Through analysing customer Know Your Customer (KYC) information and money laundering cases, the company is able to build customer profiles with higher probability of money laundering and rigorously vet such customers before onboarding them. Analytics in risk management will also enable the company to quantify risk exposures and identify controls that result in enhanced risk adjusted returns. Through the improvements in its assurance functions, the company will reduce operational losses and regulatory breaches.

iii.question: Discuss the risks that Company C needs to consider before making a significant investment in its data analytics and predictive modelling capabilities.

Indicative Solution: Costs and benefits: Anticipated future benefits from investment in data analytics capabilities not materialising. There is risk that the costs of running the data analytics and predictive modelling capabilities are more than the intended benefits. This would make the company lose value from the process. Ultimate costs of investment in data analytics capabilities may be much greater than expected. Implementation taking longer and being much more complex and expensive to implement than expected.

Data Integrity: The company is exposed to the risk that the data required for data analytics and predictive modelling isn't of the required quality. This is due to the garbage in garbage out situation if the data is of poor quality. Cases of poor quality may include: Incorrect customer information; Missing information about customers/ blank spaces on customer Know Your Customers (KYC) mandates; Data formats that aren't compatible for the data analytics and predictive modelling tools to be used; Data may still be in hard copy format; Incorrect policy and claims data; Policy and claims data use different data formats. The insights derived from such data will be incorrect and management may make decisions that harm the company using such insights. Examples of decisions that may harm the company in the future include the following: Adopting product design that doesn't meet customer needs; Changes in pricing that reduce business volumes or reduce profitability for the company; In-appropriate loyalty rewards for customers leading to reputational risk; Keeping lower than optimal reserves for the company; Underwriting decisions may be against customers who should have been accepted or accepting customers who should have been declined or whose premiums should have been loaded.

Data Privacy: The company will be exposed to the risk of breaking data privacy laws which can attract regulatory fines or negative media coverage. Some customers may not be willing to have their data analysed and used to make decisions that may be against them. Hence they may feel that the company is now encroaching into their privacy. However, this can be managed by declaring to customers how the company uses their data and seeking signed consent from customers to use their data accordingly. Consultations with the company's legal team (internal and external) will be needed to ensure all data privacy laws have been complied with so as to minimize potential of regulatory fines.

Data Storage: The company must consider the data storage requirements for the data analytics and predictive modelling capabilities it wants to build. If the company underestimates the data storage requirements, it will have to procure data storage capabilities at much higher cost in the future which would affect profitability of the company. In the same vein, loss of data or shutdown of operating systems may occur once data storage is exhausted. This would lead to business interruption and potential loss of business. On the other side, overestimating data storage requirements will lead to underutilisation of capacity and sunk costs which also affect returns for the company. Optimisation of the data storage capabilities will ensure that the company gets the benefits of the data analytics and predictive modelling without overspending on the capabilities thus boosting returns for the company.

Information Security: The company will also be exposed to the risk of its systems being hacked and its data being leaked to external parties. The company can then lose material amounts of money due to ransom or loss of business after reputational damage from the data leakage. Regulatory scrutiny and fines may also arise due to loss of data. Therefore, the company must consider the investments it must make to improve its data security environment before investing in data analytics and predictive modelling.

Data Literacy: Whilst the company may develop the appropriate tools for data analytics and predictive modelling, if data literacy is too low within its employees, they won't utilize the insights. Therefore, there is the risk that the benefits of data analytics and predictive modelling won't be realized due to data literacy. In the same vein, weak data culture results in management and executives continuing to rely on their own personal experiences, biases and preferences at the expense of data informed decision making. Literacy also includes the required specialist personnel who will be carrying out the data analytics and predictive modelling, hence there is risk of recruiting personnel that fail to deliver the expected results. The required skills may be difficult to obtain, or disproportionately expensive, due to high demand in the market. Hence, the company also needs to assess the current state of data literacy and data culture within the company and put in place plans to improve data literacy and culture to the level that can support maximum exploitation of data analytics and predictive modelling.

Regulation: Regulation may prohibit use of certain data or personal information to determine the pricing or risk acceptance per customer. This may limit the extent to which the company can utilize the insights it builds. Therefore, the company needs to consider regulatory requirements and focus on capabilities that can be applied to the day-to-day running of the business.

Other: Company not having the expertise required to implement a project like this. Reputational damage if project does not deliver expected benefits. There is the risk of accidental / unintentional discrimination due to use of data analytics without care.

4.label: Question 4
4.scenario:
Company A is a medium-sized health and care insurance company that was launched 5 years ago, specialising in individual and group Income Protection (IP) products.

The current reinsurance arrangements for Company A are as follows:

Individual IP:
• Quota share treaty arrangement on a risk premium basis, with a retention level of 0% (i.e. 100% of the morbidity risk is reinsured).
• Facultative reinsurance arrangements that are individually negotiable on a case-by-case basis.

Group IP:
• Excess of loss treaty with an excess point of £100,000 per life and £1 million per scheme. There is an upper limit of £5 million per scheme.
• Aggregate excess of loss with an excess point of £20 million.
• Catastrophe excess of loss reinsurance.

i.question: Set out the rationale for the specific reinsurance arrangements mentioned above, from the perspective of Company A.

Indicative Solution: (a) Individual IP - Quota share reinsurance:

Capital Efficiency: This allows Company A to free up capital for other business activities, such as expanding their product lines or entering new markets. Ceding a fixed percentage of premiums rather than losses or policies can be an efficient use of capital for the insurer. This approach ensures that capital is not tied up in underwriting high volumes of policies or excessive exposure while still allowing the insurer to write business. This enables medium sized insurer to underwrite more income protection policies and grow their market share without taking on excessive risk.

Enhancing Solvency: Company A may be facing potential solvency or capital adequacy challenges due to the accumulation of high-risk income protection policies, by ceding these policies on an individual facultative basis, insurer can improve their overall solvency position.

Stabilising Profitability: Income protection insurance often experiences fluctuations in profitability due to factors like claims variability. Quota share reinsurance can stabilise, insurer's financial results by sharing the impact of these fluctuations with reinsurers. Reinsuring 100% effectively means that Company A is not exposed to any morbidity risk. This would be a good strategy to build up morbidity experience for a new company.

Strategic Partnerships and Technical Support: Risk premium-based quota share reinsurance can foster strong partnerships between insurers and reinsurers. By sharing in the premiums, both parties are motivated to work closely together to underwrite and price risks effectively. Technical support - As a new company, Company A may require significant technical support from its reinsurers and one of the requirements may be a high proportion of reinsurance to make it worthwhile for the reinsurers.
Facultative reinsurance:

Risk Management and Flexibility: Facultative reinsurance provides insurer the flexibility to selectively cede individual policies or specific portions of their income protection portfolio. This allows insurer to manage its risk exposure more precisely, especially when dealing with high-value or non-standard policies.

Tailored Coverage: Income protection products can vary significantly in terms of coverage, terms, and policyholder characteristics. Facultative reinsurance enables insurer to tailor the reinsurance coverage to match the specific attributes and risk profiles of individual policies. This customisation can be particularly valuable for unique or complex cases.

Risk Selection and Pricing Precision: Facultative reinsurance allows insurer to select and cede policies with a higher risk profile or uncertain underwriting characteristics. Reinsurers can assess these risks on a case-by-case basis and offer terms and pricing that accurately reflect the risk, which helps insurer maintain appropriate pricing and profitability.

Claim Management: Facultative reinsurance can be useful in managing claims on complex or high-value income protection policies. Reinsurers often have the resources and expertise to handle claims more efficiently, which can benefit both the insurer and the policyholder.

Expanding Capacity: Reinsurers may have a deeper understanding of specialised risks, enabling them to provide valuable insights and risk assessment for individual income protection policies. Insurer may use facultative reinsurance to expand their capacity to underwrite income protection policies beyond their normal underwriting limits. This enables them to take on larger policies and attract more business.

Protecting Profit Margins: Facultative reinsurance can help protect the profitability of an income protection portfolio by allowing insurer to offload high-risk or problematic policies while retaining the more profitable ones.

Risk Mitigation: Facultative reinsurance can be a valuable risk mitigation tool in situations where insurer want to limit exposure to certain policyholder characteristics, occupational hazards, or other factors that can impact the risk profile of income protection policies.
(b) Group IP - Individual excess of loss:

Risk Mitigation: Concentration of risk - Group Income Protection products often cover a large number of policyholders within an organisation. Aggregate excess of loss reinsurance helps Company A mitigate the risk of high frequency, low-severity claims that can collectively impact profitability.

Enhanced Profitability: By limiting its exposure to large claims, Company A can protect its profitability. Excess of loss reinsurance helps insurer maintain its profit margins, as the reinsurer takes on the financial burden of claims exceeding the specified retention level.

Capital Efficiency: Excess of loss reinsurance optimizes capital efficiency by allowing insurer to allocate its capital more effectively. Company A can use the capital that would otherwise be set aside for large claims to expand its business or invest in other areas.

Solvency Protection: In cases where a significant number of policyholders are covered under group income protection, the risk of insolvency due to a sudden surge in claims is reduced. The reinsurer absorbs the financial impact of such events, helping to protect the insurer's solvency.

Competitive Advantage: Offering excess of loss reinsurance can make insurer more competitive in the market for group income protection products. This reinsurance arrangement allows insurer to provide clients with coverage for large claims while keeping premiums reasonable.

Risk Management: Excess of loss reinsurance can be a key component of an insurer's overall risk management strategy. It enables insurer to manage its exposure to large claims more effectively and maintain a healthy balance between risk retention and risk transfer.

Aggregate and Catastrophic excess of loss: Group policies can be vulnerable to catastrophic events, such as a widespread economic downturn or a pandemic, leading to a surge in claims. Excess of loss reinsurance provides protection against these large-scale and potentially financially devastating events.

ii.question:
Company A has experienced significant business growth over the last 5 years. The Finance Director has proposed the following changes to Company A's reinsurance strategy for all future business written under its individual IP portfolio:

• Increase the quota share retention level from 0% to 50% (i.e. 50% of morbidity risk will be reinsured).
• 'Nil reinsurance premium period' where for each new policy reinsured, free reinsurance cover is to be provided in the first 2 years, followed by level reinsurance premiums thereafter.

Discuss the possible rationale behind the Finance Director's proposals.

Indicative Solution: (a) Reducing the reinsurance proportion:

Retain Profit Margins: By retaining a higher percentage of Income Protection policies and the associated premiums, insurer can retain more of its profit margins. This can be particularly beneficial if the insurer has experienced improved underwriting results, lower claims payouts and significant business growth. Reinsurance premium rates may have increased and hence the benefit of reinsurance has become less attractive.

Diversification of Risk: Reducing the quota share reinsurance proportion may be part of a strategy to diversify the insurer's concentration of reinsurance risk. This can involve retaining certain segments of income protection products while ceding others, which can balance the risk profile.

Risk Retention and Reward: Company A may want to retain a larger share of the risk to enjoy the full financial benefits of its underwriting expertise. By reducing reinsurance, it can reap the rewards of successful risk management and more profitable policy portfolios.

Improved Underwriting Insights: Company A may now have sufficient insights to perform its own underwriting, reducing its reinsurance proportion and reliance on reinsurers' services thus help save costs.

Client Relationships: Clients may have preferences for insurers that retain a larger portion of the risk, as this can demonstrate the insurer's confidence in its underwriting and claims handling capabilities.
Strategic Goals: Company A may use reinsurance to meet their strategic goals to expand its income protection business, diversify its revenue streams, or meet specific financial targets.

Capital Requirements: Regulatory and solvency requirements may have evolved and reducing the reinsurance proportion may help Company A align with changing capital adequacy regulations. This can help ensure compliance with capital requirements and reduce the reliance on reinsurers to meet these standards, in particular that the regulatory changes lead to lower gross of reinsurance capital requirements, or higher net of reinsurance capital requirements due to reduction in the allowance for reinsurance benefit.

Adaptation to Market Conditions: Changing market conditions, such as shifts in interest rates or economic stability, can influence the attractiveness of quota share reinsurance. Company A may adjust the proportion to adapt to the current market environment.
(b) Nil reinsurance premium period:

Capital Efficiency: Nil reinsurance premium period will help Company A alleviate initial capital stain as no reinsurance premiums will be payable in the first two years. This arrangement would be particularly effective if the two years period is aligned to Company A's initial commission period. Depending on reserving requirement, the nil reinsurance premium feature may help reduce initial reserving and capital requirements.

Protection against persistency risk: A nil reinsurance period effectively passes persistency risk in the first two years to the reinsurer, in particular within the period that initial commission are being recouped.

Attractive reinsurance terms: Reinsurance premiums post the nil reinsurance premium period are likely to be higher than level reinsurance premiums from the outset, but the overall terms may still be more attractive. The nil reinsurance premium feature may only now be available in the reinsurance market which was not previously available.

iii.question: Describe the investigations that Company A should take to assess the appropriateness of increasing the retention level from 0% to 50%.

Indicative Solution: Investigation: Analyse historical loss data under existing IP products to identify trends and patterns. The credibility of the existing experience data should be considered carefully as there is only 5 years of experience. Analyse the characteristics of the insurance portfolio, including the type of risks, geographic location, and the historical loss experience. Understanding the risk profile is essential in determining how much risk to cede. This information can help in setting a reinsurance percentage that aligns with insurer's risk management strategy.

Steps to determine appropriate proportion: Use financial modelling techniques to project the potential impact of different reinsurance percentages on insurer's financial performance. This can include evaluating scenarios with different quota share percentages. Conduct sensitivity analysis to assess how changes in the quota share percentage might impact insurer's financial results under different scenarios. Take into account the diversification benefits of the reinsurance arrangement. A quota share reinsurance arrangement can provide diversification by spreading the risk across multiple reinsurers. Engage in negotiations with potential reinsurers to determine the final quota share percentage, taking into account pricing, terms, and conditions. Ensure that the agreement aligns with company's own objectives. After implementing the reinsurance arrangement, continuously monitor and review its effectiveness. Adjust the quota share percentage, if necessary, based on evolving circumstances and performance.

Other considerations: Consider regulatory and capital requirements that may influence the percentage of risk Company A can cede. These requirements can vary by jurisdiction and type of insurance. Determine insurer's risk tolerance, which is the maximum level of risk the company is willing to retain after reinsurance. This will help establish a basis for deciding the percentage to cede. Assess the capacity and willingness of reinsurers to take on insurer's risks. Insurer needs to negotiate with potential reinsurers to determine the available capacity and pricing. The quota share reinsurance percentage is a strategic decision that should align with insurer's risk management and financial goals.

5.label: Question 5
5.scenario:
Company B is a large bancassurer that sells a range of health and care products including conventional (non-linked) and unit-linked Critical Illness (CI) insurance products.

For the conventional CI product, premiums are guaranteed throughout the policy term. CI benefit for each individual policyholder is the amount of sum assured selected at the start of the policy term. This product does not offer a surrender benefit if the policyholders terminate their policies before the end of the policy term.

For the unit-linked CI product, policyholders can choose from a range of Company B's unit-linked funds (e.g. equity, property or fixed interest security funds) to invest their premiums to build up their individual unit funds. Investment returns on policyholders' unit funds are directly linked to the investment performance of Company B's unit-linked funds. The CI benefit for each individual policyholder is either the amount of sum assured selected by the policyholders at the start of the policy term or the value of the unit fund at the claim inception date, whichever is higher. This product also offers a surrender benefit equivalent to the value of the unit fund at the termination date without a surrender penalty. Morbidity charges are deducted from policyholders' unit funds to cover the cost of the CI benefits. Additional expense charges are deducted to cover the cost of administration. Both morbidity and expense charges are reviewable, with the first review on each policy's fifth anniversary, which are followed by annual reviews thereafter.

i.question: Discuss the advantages and disadvantages of each type of CI product from the perspective of Company B.

Indicative Solution: Non-linked Critical Illness - Advantages: Non-linked Critical Illness products with guaranteed premiums over the policy term, providing Company B with a stable and predictable stream of premium income. The insurer's financial obligations are primarily based on underwriting and claims experience. Non-linked products are simpler to design, underwrite, and administer compared to unit-linked or investment-linked products, reducing administrative and operational complexities. The absence of investment elements makes it easier to communicate the product features, terms, and conditions to policyholders, reducing the risk of misunderstandings or disputes. Some of Company B's customers may prefer simple (relatively low cost) CI product, and therefore this product will meet this customer segment's needs.

Non-linked Critical Illness - Disadvantages: Non-linked products do not generate additional investment income for Company B, and its profitability is solely based on underwriting and claims experience. This can result in lower profit margins compared to linked products. The non-linked Critical Illness market can be highly competitive, with many insurers offering similar products. Company B may need to compete on price or features to attract and retain customers. Compliance and reporting obligations can increase administrative costs. For customers seeking both insurance coverage and an investment or savings element, non-linked products may not meet their needs, potentially leading to a smaller customer base. While non-linked products focus on critical illness coverage, insurers may still be exposed to mortality risk if they offer death benefits as part of the product (e.g. accelerated critical illness), which could result in higher claim costs. Non-linked Critical Illness policies with guaranteed premiums often involve long-term commitments. This limits an insurer's flexibility to adapt to changing market conditions or regulatory requirements.

Unit-linked Critical Illness - Advantages: The main advantage is the reviewable feature as morbidity charges are not guaranteed. Unit-linked products generate income from investment charges (annual management charges or investment fees) as well as risk premiums, allowing insurers to diversify their revenue streams beyond traditional insurance policies. These products can be appealing to customers seeking both insurance coverage and investment opportunities, potentially attracting a broader customer base. The investment component transfers a portion of the financial risk to policyholders, reducing the insurer's overall exposure to market fluctuations. Insurer can offer a range of investment options and flexibility in policy design, enabling Company B to cater to different customer preferences and risk appetites.

Unit-linked Critical Illness - Disadvantages: Company B may face indirect investment risk associated with the performance of the underlying investment funds, and adverse market conditions can lead to financial losses for the insurer. For example, fund management charges collected as a percentage of fund may be inadequate to cover expenses. Unit-linked products can be more complex to design and administer, requiring specialised expertise in both insurance and investment management. Insurer must adhere to stringent regulatory requirements and disclosure standards, which may involve ongoing compliance costs and reporting obligations. The premium income from unit-linked policies can be more variable due to fluctuations in investment markets, impacting the insurer's cash flow and profitability. Balancing the insurance and investment components while ensuring fairness to policyholders can be challenging, potentially leading to disputes or regulatory scrutiny. Insurer must ensure that unit-linked products are sold responsibly and that policyholders fully understand the risks and benefits, as there is a potential for mis-selling and disputes. Reputational damage issues for insurer if unit-linked funds perform worse than expected. In certain circumstances, insurer may face liquidity risks if a large number of policyholders decide to surrender their policies or withdraw investments simultaneously. Potential customers complaints if the review leads to an increase in premium or reduction in sum assured.

ii.question: Set out the factors that Company B should consider when reviewing the morbidity charges for its unit-linked CI products.

Indicative Solution: Key initial factors to take into account in the review would be crucially dictated by the terms and conditions of the policy, as well as the local regulatory landscape. When reviewing morbidity charges for unit-linked Critical Illness (CI) products, Company B should consider several factors to ensure that the pricing and charges are appropriate and reflect the actual risk and costs associated with providing CI coverage.

Evaluate the insurer's historical morbidity experience for the unit-linked CI products. Assess whether actual claims experience aligns with the pricing assumptions made at the product's inception. If morbidity claims are higher than expected, it may indicate that the charges need to be adjusted to cover the increased risk (and vice versa).

Examine the underwriting criteria and processes used to assess the health and risk profiles of policyholders. If the underwriting criteria have changed or if there is a significant deviation between the underwritten risks and the actual claims experience, it may be necessary to adjust the morbidity charges.

Analyse the demographics of policyholders, including age, gender, occupation, and other relevant factors. Changes in the policyholder mix can influence morbidity rates and should be considered when reviewing charges.

Study the trends in CI claims, such as the types of critical illnesses being claimed, their severity, and the age at which claims are being made. Identifying changing claims patterns can help insurer adjust morbidity charges accordingly. Experience should be inspected for any emerging trends, eg relating to advances in medical treatment and diagnostic technologies which can impact morbidity experience. If medical advancements lead to better diagnosis and treatment of critical illnesses, it may result in more claims, affecting the adequacy of morbidity charges.

Compare the emerging experience with the original pricing assumptions, including morbidity rates and policyholder behaviour. Changes in these assumptions, such as lower morbidity rates due to improved health awareness or higher claim severity, should be taken into account.

Consider the performance of the investment component of the unit-linked CI products. It would not normally be allowable to adjust morbidity charges in response to other poor experience, such as lower investment returns, unless it is explicitly covered by the product's terms and conditions.

Economic conditions can influence retention/lapse rates. Insurer should review the economic environment and its potential impact on the ability of policyholders to afford premiums and maintain their policies.
Analyse the competitive landscape for unit-linked CI products. Pricing should be competitive while adequately covering the insurer's costs and ensuring profitability.

Review the insurer's profitability targets and financial goals for the unit-linked CI products. Ensure that the morbidity charges align with these objectives while providing fair value to policyholders.
Collect and consider feedback from policyholders regarding the pricing and value of the CI coverage. Address any concerns or issues raised by policyholders to enhance customer satisfaction.
The distribution channel through which unit-linked CI products are sold can influence pricing and charges. Evaluate the impact of distribution costs and fees on the overall pricing structure.
